policy_statement: Propose regional targets: 50 Good Food shops and 100 Good Food label restaurants.
mechanism_description: The city is proposing regional targets to increase the number of “Good Food” shops and “Good Food” label restaurants, supporting a shift toward lower-emissions food options and reduced food-system emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Food consumption/procurement shift → ↓ high-emissions food production + ↓ supply-chain emissions → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in system/human activity: City increases availability and uptake of “Good Food” certified shops and restaurants, influencing menus, sourcing, and consumer choices toward lower-emissions foods. (2) Mediating physical/process mechanisms: Reduced demand for emissions-intensive products (often ruminant meat/dairy) and/or more efficient, lower-input production and distribution (potentially including reduced food waste) decreases upstream agricultural methane/nitrous oxide and energy-related CO₂ in processing/transport. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle food-system GHG emissions attributable to city food consumption and procurement patterns.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced food-system greenhouse gas emissions by shifting demand and supply toward lower-emissions food options.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: 1) Dominant causal pathway: a consumption/supply shift in the urban food system leading to lower lifecycle GHG emissions (mitigation). 2) Why this primary over alternatives: while “sustainable food” can have resource-efficiency co-benefits (less waste, lower inputs) and occasional nature-based elements, the central, largest climate effect described is direct GHG emissions reduction from changing what food is sold/served. 3) Edge cases applied: if the program were primarily about reducing food waste, it could lean toward Resource Efficiency; if primarily about regenerative agriculture/carbon sequestration, it could lean toward Nature-Based Solutions—but those are not the stated dominant mechanism here (it is outlet/label expansion to shift offerings). 4) Applicability to ALL instances of this mechanism: any policy framed as expanding sustainable/low-carbon food programs to reduce food-system emissions shares the same dominant mitigation pathway via demand/supply change and associated lifecycle emissions reductions. 5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., plant-forward diets vs. local sourcing vs. waste reduction) is deferred to policy-level evidence and is not determined at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential ambiguity if “Good Food” criteria emphasize local/seasonal or waste reduction more than lifecycle GHG reduction; local sourcing is not always lower-carbon, and waste-focused implementations could shift the dominant effect toward Resource Efficiency. If the program explicitly drives regenerative practices that increase soil carbon, a Nature-Based Solutions secondary pathway may be significant, but not dominant as described.
key_indicators: “proposing that the region should have 50 Good Food shops”; “proposing that the region reach 100 restaurants with the Good Food label”; “Increase the number of Good Food”
co_benefits: Improved public health/nutrition; support for local/ethical food supply chains; reduced packaging via bulk products; potential waste reduction.
instance_edge_case_notes: Targets are framed as a proposal (“proposing that the region should/reach”), so it is unclear whether they are binding commitments, part of a formal plan, or contingent on incentives/partnerships not shown in the excerpt.
